full_response:
Below is a self‐contained derivation.  We use units with G=c=1.

1)  Define the “tortoise” coordinate  
   r*≡r+2M ln|r/2M−1|,  
   and null coordinates  
   u≡t−r*, v≡t+r*.  

2)  The Kruskal null coordinates are  
   U=−e^{−u/(4M)}, V= e^{ v/(4M)}.  

3)  The condition r>2M (the two exterior regions) in Kruskal is  
   UV<0.  Moreover the two disjoint components are  
     Region I (the “right” exterior):   U<0, V>0  
     Region III (the “left” exterior): U>0, V<0  

4)  As r→∞ (both asymptotic ends) we have r*→+∞, so for finite t,  
     u=t−r*→−∞ ⇒ U→−∞,  
     v=t+r*→+∞ ⇒ V→+∞,  
   in Region I (U<0,V>0),  
   and by crossing the throat one goes to Region III where U>0, V<0 and again |U|,|V|→∞.  

Hence the two asymptotic regions (the two ends of the Einstein–Rosen bridge) in (U,V)‐space are simply the two disconnected regions of r>2M, namely  

 Region I: U<0, V>0  
 Region III: U>0, V<0.  

\boxed{\text{The two exterior (asymptotic) regions are given by }(U<0,\;V>0)\text{ and }(U>0,\;V<0)\,.}

Confidence: 95%
extracted_answer: \text{The two exterior (asymptotic) regions are given by }(U<0,\;V>0)\text{ and }(U>0,\;V<0)\,.
